rationale: VDA ISA 9.1.1 requires a documented data protection policy; BDSG §38(1) mandates DPO appointment for private-sector organisations with ≥20 persons in automated processing, implying a governance structure but not requiring a written policy document per se — the policy sub-requirement is narrower.

The policy must reflect mandatory DPO appointment triggers (DPIA-obligatory processing, commercial data trading) per BDSG §38(2); policy scope and DPO mandate scope overlap but neither fully subsumes the other.

Where the data protection policy covers employee data processing, it must reflect the BDSG §26(1) lawfulness condition (employment-relationship necessity); automotive supplier context involves employees processing OEM data, creating partial overlap.

VDA ISA 9.2.1 requires designation of a qualified DPO or equivalent privacy responsible; BDSG §38(1) is the German-specific DPO appointment obligation (20-person threshold) that co-extensively covers the same designation requirement for private bodies.

VDA ISA 9.2.1 requires the DPO to be protected from dismissal and conflicts of interest; BDSG §6(4) (applied to private bodies via §38) prohibits dismissal or disadvantage for performance of DPO tasks, directly co-extensive.

VDA ISA 9.2.1 lists DPO tasks (advising, monitoring, DPIA cooperation); BDSG §7 specifies public-body DPO tasks, applied analogously to private bodies via §38 — source is a subset as BDSG §7 also covers cooperation with supervisory authority.

VDA ISA 9.3.1 mandates a Record of Processing Activities (RoPA); BDSG §38(1) is related because organisations below the DPO threshold still require a RoPA under GDPR Art. 30 — BDSG does not separately legislate the RoPA for private non-public bodies (GDPR Art. 30 applies directly), so the link is contextual rather than direct.

Where the RoPA documents employee data processing activities, the legal basis entry must reflect BDSG §26(1) (employment necessity); the VDA requirement to record legal bases is a subset of §26(1)'s broader employment data lawfulness regime.

VDA ISA 9.3.1 requires recording consent-based processing activities; BDSG §26(2) imposes form, voluntariness and documentation requirements for employee consent — VDA records the existence of consent but §26(2) adds materiality conditions beyond mere recording.

Where the RoPA covers special-category data (health, biometric, union membership), BDSG §22(2) requires additional safeguards to be documented; VDA ISA 9.3.1 captures the processing activity, §22(2) adds the safeguard-documentation obligation — partial overlap at the record level.

VDA ISA 9.4.1 requires a DPIA for high-risk processing; BDSG §38(2) makes DPO appointment mandatory where Art. 35 GDPR DPIA is required — both provisions are triggered by the same high-risk processing threshold, making the VDA criterion and §38(2) trigger co-extensive.

VDA ISA 9.4.1 requires DPIA for processing special categories (Art. 9 GDPR); BDSG §22(2) requires appropriate and specific measures when processing special categories — VDA DPIA is one such measure but §22(2) mandates a broader set of technical and organisational safeguards.

DPIA obligations under 9.4.1 apply where employee monitoring or profiling occurs; BDSG §26(1) governs lawfulness of employment data processing including monitoring measures — related because DPIA necessity often arises from §26(1) processing at scale.

VDA ISA 9.5.1 requires data processing agreements (AVV) for commissioned processors; BDSG §24(1) restricts further processing by private non-public bodies for incompatible purposes — AVV governs scope of permissible processing, §24(1) adds a purpose-limitation restriction layer that extends beyond the contract obligation.

Where AVV covers processors handling employee data (e.g. HR SaaS), BDSG §26(4) extends employee data protections to processing by works-council bodies — VDA criterion requires the contract; §26(4) adds a substantive constraint on contractual scope.

VDA ISA 9.5.2 requires transfer impact assessments for data transfers to sub-processors in third countries; BDSG §25(1) permits public-body transfers abroad only under listed conditions — VDA is narrower (private automotive context); §25(1) covers public body transfers with additional regulatory conditions.

Third-country transfer controls in 9.5.2 must ensure the transfer purpose is compatible with the original purpose; BDSG §24(1) prohibits non-public body processing for incompatible purposes — same compatibility principle applies to cross-border onward transfers.

VDA ISA 9.5.3 addresses intra-group and special-category cross-border transfers; BDSG §25(1) is the public-body analogue — for private automotive suppliers the GDPR standard safeguards (SCCs/BCRs) apply directly, with §25 as a reference point for adequacy-equivalent conditions.

VDA ISA 9.5.3 requires heightened safeguards when transferring special-category data (Art. 9) internationally; BDSG §22(2) independently requires specific measures for special-category processing — both mandate documented protective measures co-extensively for this data category.

VDA ISA 9.6.1 requires processes to handle data subject information requests (Art. 13/14 GDPR); BDSG §32(1) specifies the information-obligation content when collecting data from the subject directly — VDA operationalises the procedure §32 prescribes, making them co-extensive at the obligation level.

VDA ISA 9.6.1 covers information obligations where data is collected indirectly (e.g. from OEM); BDSG §33(1) sets out information to be provided when data is not collected from the data subject — both cover the same indirect-collection transparency obligation.

VDA ISA 9.6.1 requires a defined process for access requests; BDSG §34(1) specifies the access right (Auskunftsrecht) including restrictions for non-public bodies — both address the same right; VDA adds a procedural maturity dimension (response within deadlines) that §34 does not prescribe.

VDA ISA 9.6.1 process scope includes erasure requests; BDSG §35(1) supplements GDPR Art. 17 erasure right with a German-specific restriction for non-automated files — VDA obligation to handle erasure is co-extensive, with §35(1) adding a specific analogue-file caveat.

VDA ISA 9.6.1 includes objection requests in the data-subject-rights process; BDSG §36(1) limits the objection right vis-à-vis public bodies for overriding public interest — for private automotive suppliers this operates as a GDPR Art. 21 supplement, making the VDA process requirement co-extensive.

Where VDA ISA 9.6.1 process covers automated-decision opt-out requests, BDSG §37(1) supplements GDPR Art. 22 on automated individual decisions — related because TISAX automotive context rarely involves fully automated decisions but the handler process must cater for this.

VDA ISA 9.6.2 requires a data breach response process including internal and external notification; BDSG §43(1) establishes administrative fines for breaches of specific processing obligations — VDA control establishes the preventive/detective procedure; §43 is the enforcement backstop rather than a process specification, so VDA is a subset of the broader compliance regime.

VDA ISA 9.6.2 requires breach notification procedures; BDSG §42(2) establishes criminal liability for unauthorised disclosure of personal data — the VDA incident process is preventive; §42(2) is the criminal sanction for intentional or negligent breach, related as deterrent backdrop.

VDA ISA 9.6.2 requires DPO involvement in breach response; BDSG §38(1) mandates DPO appointment which in turn creates the DPO role that must be notified — the breach process in VDA presupposes §38(1) DPO existence.

VDA ISA 9.7.1 requires data protection training and awareness for all employees handling personal data; BDSG §26(1) is narrower — it legitimises processing for employment purposes but does not independently mandate training programmes, so VDA training obligation exceeds §26(1) in scope.

VDA ISA 9.7.1 training obligation on confidentiality mirrors §53 Datengeheimnis (data secrecy) in the law-enforcement part of BDSG; while §53 applies to public law-enforcement bodies, it articulates the same confidentiality-training principle that VDA extends to private automotive processors — related by normative analogy.

VDA ISA 9.7.2 requires role-based authorisation and least-privilege for employees accessing personal data; BDSG §26(2) requires voluntary and documented consent for employment-data processing when consent is the basis — partial overlap because access-control applies across all legal bases, not only consent-based ones.

VDA ISA 9.7.2 role-based access limitation is a technical implementation of the necessity principle; BDSG §26(1) requires processing to be necessary for the employment relationship — VDA operationalises the §26(1) necessity requirement at the technical-access level.

VDA ISA 9.8.1 requires processors to follow documented controller instructions; this is the contractual obligation that underpins the DPO-overseen compliance regime — §38(1) DPO mandate exists precisely because processors operating under such instructions require independent oversight, making the governance scope co-extensive.

VDA ISA 9.8.1 instructions must cover lawfulness of processing; BDSG §26(3) preserves collective-agreement rights of employee representatives to co-determine data processing rules — instructions from the controller intersect with §26(3) works-council co-determination rights when employee data is involved.

VDA ISA 9.8.1 requires documented lawful instruction basis to avoid unlawful processing; BDSG §43(2) sets administrative fines for processing without adequate legal basis — instructions control is the compliance measure; §43(2) is the sanction for absence of such control, related as enforcement backstop.

Where the RoPA includes research or statistical processing purposes (e.g. OEM quality analytics), BDSG §27(1) provides a German-specific research privilege — VDA requires documentation of purpose; §27(1) is the legal basis that must appear in that RoPA entry.

VDA ISA 9.4.1 DPIA must document technical and organisational safeguards for special-category processing; BDSG §22(2) lit. g explicitly lists designation of a DPO as a required safeguard — both converge on requiring documented protective measures including DPO designation.

VDA ISA 9.6.1 requires information to be provided within a reasonable delay when a temporary obstacle exists; BDSG §32(2) sets a two-week maximum for deferred information provision after the obstacle lapses — both operationalise the same deferred-notification deadline.

VDA ISA 9.6.1 information process must handle exceptions (e.g. confidentiality obligations); BDSG §33(2) enumerates specific exemptions from the indirect-collection information duty — VDA requires a documented exception process; §33(2) provides the exhaustive list of permissible exceptions that process must reflect.

VDA ISA 9.6.1 must handle access requests where data is subject to professional secrecy obligations; BDSG §29(1) restricts data subject rights where disclosure would harm legally protected confidentiality interests — related because automotive supplier HR data can include data under legal secrecy.

VDA ISA 9.5.1 requires contractual obligations on sub-processors regarding special-category data; BDSG §22(2) lit. d requires contractual clauses restricting access to special-category data as a mandatory safeguard — VDA AVV obligation is a subset; §22(2) lit. d adds specific contractual content requirements beyond mere existence of the agreement.

VDA ISA 9.2.1 requires DPO to report to highest management level; BDSG §38(5) references §5(3) requiring DPO to report directly to top management — both impose identical reporting-chain requirements making them co-extensive.

VDA ISA 9.1.1 data protection policy must include obligations to maintain processing records; BDSG §43(1) Nr. 7 imposes fines for failure to maintain RoPA — policy obligation and sanction norm are related as the policy establishes the compliance intent §43 enforces.
